metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'securely' in AC1 with a concrete observable definition: specify the tmpfile path (e.g. `/tmp/rebar_signing.key`), required permissions (`chmod 600`), a GHA `::add-mask::` step to prevent log exposure, and a post-job cleanup step.
findings.finding: AC1 uses the subjective qualifier 'securely' with no concrete observable definition; the key injection mechanism (tmpfile path, file permissions, GHA `mask`/`add-mask` step, and post-job cleanup) is unspecified, leaving the executor to choose an approach.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: '`reconcile-bridge-canary.yml` injects the signing key securely and sets committer email to the bot.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'Step: write key via `echo "$REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY" > /tmp/rebar_signing.key && chmod 600 /tmp/rebar_signing.key`; add a `run: echo "::add-mask::$REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY"` step before any use; add a post step to `rm -f /tmp/rebar_signing.key`. AC1 passes when a log scan (`grep -i signing_key <run-log>`) returns no matches.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC2 with a full proving command: specify how to trigger a canary run (e.g. `workflow_dispatch` on `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml`), how to capture the created artifact ID, and the exact `verify-identity` invocation with expected output (e.g. `rebar verify-identity --ticket <ID> | jq -e '.signer == "Rebar Bot"'`).
findings.finding: AC2 references `verify-identity` as a proving command but does not specify the full invocation, the artifact to pass to it (e.g. ticket/comment ID from a triggered canary run), or the expected output, so it cannot be evaluated without guessing.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'A canary-created bug/comment/transition is signed as Rebar Bot (verifiable via `verify-identity`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's framing: `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` is a NEW committer email being introduced (replacing the current default `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com`), not 'the current bridge default'.
findings.finding: The plan describes the committer email `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` as 'from the current bridge default', but the actual current default in `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml` is `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com` (via `vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL || 'rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com'`). The email is not the current default — it is a new value being introduced. This false premise could cause an implementer to underestimate scope (thinking no email change is needed) or omit the email flip step entirely.
findings.location: Plan body: 'Set the committer email to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` (from the current bridge default)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'from the current bridge default' with 'replacing the current default `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com`' to make clear this is a change, not a pre-existing state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `reconcile-bridge.yml` as a consumer of `vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` and explicitly state whether the email flip also applies there or must be scoped to the canary job only (e.g., hardcode the email in the canary step rather than via a shared variable).
findings.finding: `reconcile-bridge.yml` is an unenumerated consumer of `vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL`. The canary's 'Configure git identity' step reads `vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL`; the sibling `reconcile-bridge.yml` reads the same repo-level variable at lines 196 and 235. If the plan's email flip is implemented by updating the `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` repo variable, `reconcile-bridge.yml` is also affected and should be listed as a co-changed or explicitly acknowledged consumer.
findings.location: Plan body: 'Set the committer email to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`'; `.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml` lines 196, 235
findings.suggested_fix: Hardcode `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` directly in the canary's 'Configure git identity' step (overriding the variable only for that job) rather than changing the shared `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` variable, to avoid unintended side-effects on `reconcile-bridge.yml`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the config mechanism for runtime key injection: specify that `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` env var (the documented override in `_config_schema.py`) is used in the workflow step rather than a static `rebar.toml` edit, so the 'no code change / signs automatically' claim is concretely satisfied.
findings.finding: The plan says 'set `identity.signing_key` to [the injected file]' but `identity.signing_key` in `rebar.toml` is a committed static value — a per-run temp-file path cannot be written there. The only verified runtime injection mechanism is the env var `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` (documented in `_config_schema.py` line 314, used in tests). The plan does not name this mechanism, leaving the core capability assertion — that signing will activate automatically with no code change — unresolved at the implementation level.
findings.suggested_fix: In the workflow step, export `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` pointing to the injected tmpfile (e.g., `echo "${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}" > /tmp/rebar_bot_key && chmod 600 /tmp/rebar_bot_key` then `env: REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY: /tmp/rebar_bot_key` on the rebar CLI steps), rather than editing `rebar.toml`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact mechanism for setting `identity.signing_key` at GHA runtime — specifically call out `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY=<tmpfile-path>` as the env var override, rather than the ambiguous 'set identity.signing_key to it'.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the mechanism by which `identity.signing_key` is set to the injected file path. The config key can be set via `rebar.toml` (static) or the env override `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` (documented in `_config_schema.py` line 314). At CI runtime, only the env var path is viable (mutating `rebar.toml` would create a dirty commit), but the plan is silent on this — leaving the implementer to discover the env var or attempt an unsupported runtime toml edit.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a workflow step: `echo "${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}" > /tmp/rebar-bot-signing-key && chmod 0600 /tmp/rebar-bot-signing-key` and set `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY: /tmp/rebar-bot-signing-key` in the job-level `env:` block.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact configuration mechanism for `identity.signing_key` in the GHA job (i.e., set env var `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` to the injected tempfile path, rather than writing a static path to `rebar.toml`).
findings.finding: The plan says 'set `identity.signing_key` to it' (the injected file) without specifying the configuration mechanism. The codebase provides two distinct paths: (a) `identity.signing_key` in `rebar.toml` (a committed static file — cannot hold a per-run temp path), and (b) the `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` environment variable override. The plan names neither, so the processing protocol for wiring the key into the signing seam is undefined.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'set `identity.signing_key` to it' with: 'Export `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` pointing to the injected tempfile (the env-var override documented in `_config_schema.py`); do not modify `rebar.toml`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the 'key never logged/committed' sub-criterion, e.g. 'After a canary run, confirm `grep -i REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY <run-log>` returns only masked `***` entries, and `git log -p | grep -i signing_key` returns no hits.'
findings.finding: AC3 'key never logged/committed' has no in-session proving command; there is no described step to scan GHA run logs or git history for accidental key exposure.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'No code change required here is introduced; key never logged/committed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the signing key's full runner-filesystem lifecycle in the plan: either switch to env-var injection (`REBAR_SIGNING_KEY` env var, eliminating file materialization) or, if file materialization is retained, specify 0600 permissions, a GHA `add-mask` step, and a post-job cleanup step — and update AC1 with these concrete controls instead of the opaque 'securely'.
findings.finding: The plan chooses file-materialization for the signing key ('inject to a job-local file') but does not declare the file's permission mode (0600/owner-only), whether a GHA `add-mask` step is used to prevent accidental log echoing of the path or value, or whether a post-job cleanup step removes the file — leaving the secret lifecycle on the runner filesystem undeclared.
findings.location: Plan body: 'Inject secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY to a job-local file; set identity.signing_key to it.' / AC line: 'injects the signing key securely'
findings.suggested_fix: Prefer the env-var path: set `env: REBAR_SIGNING_KEY: ${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}` on the rebar CLI steps (matching the documented shared-deployment injection pattern). This avoids file-system materialization entirely, renders the file-lifecycle question moot, and satisfies AC3's 'never logged/committed' constraint via GHA's built-in secret masking.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit guard (e.g., a `run: test -n "$REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY" || { echo 'ERROR: signing key secret missing'; exit 1; }` step) and/or an observability hook (step summary / workflow annotation) so a missing or empty signing key causes a loud, diagnosable failure rather than silent unsigned output.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new failure point (injecting `secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` to a job-local file) but specifies no behavior or observability when the secret is absent/empty — the canary could silently produce unsigned tickets or fail with an opaque error, with no alert or diagnostic surfaced to operators.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'key never logged/committed' / workflow injection step
findings.suggested_fix:
In `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml`, before the key-injection step add:
```yaml
- name: Validate signing key secret
  run: |
    if [ -z "${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}" ]; then
      echo "::error::REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY secret is missing or empty — canary will not sign tickets"
      exit 1
    fi
```
This ensures a loud, attributable failure rather than silent unsigned writes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `docs/jira-sync-setup.md` as a consumer requiring a documentation update if `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` default changes, or explicitly note that the email change is rebar-internal only and the doc default is unchanged.
findings.finding: `docs/jira-sync-setup.md` documents `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` default as `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com` and is an unenumerated consumer: if the plan changes the repo-level variable or workflow default, this documentation will be stale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out step for the committer email flip (e.g., 'revert email to previous value and re-run canary to confirm') so the operator can recover if downstream identity or permission mapping breaks.
findings.finding: The committer email flip from the 'current bridge default' to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` is acknowledged and justified, but no rollback or back-out mechanism is specified if the change causes downstream breakage (e.g., git-email-based identity resolution or permission mappings keyed to the old address break in production).
findings.location: Plan body — 'Set the committer email to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` (from the current bridge default)'; AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' note in the plan: 'If post-flip verification (AC2) fails or identity resolution breaks, revert `committer.email` to the previous bridge default in `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml` and re-run the canary workflow to confirm signing reverts cleanly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document whether `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` is already defined as a canonical constant (e.g., in a shared workflow env or CLAUDE.md) or confirm it will be set via a repo-level variable/secret so future email changes require a single-site update.
findings.finding: The bot committer email (`joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`) is hardcoded in the plan description rather than sourced from a shared configuration constant, creating a maintainability risk if the bot identity email changes.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Set the committer email to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pre-merge provisioning step (owner, command, timing) for `REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` in GitHub Actions secrets, and specify its rotation/retirement policy.
findings.finding: The plan assumes `secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` already exists in the GitHub Actions secrets store but never specifies who provisions this secret, when it is created, or how/when it is retired or rotated — leaving the shared-state lifecycle incomplete.
findings.location: Plan body: 'Inject `secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` to a job-local file'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an 'Prerequisites / Secret Provisioning' section: identify the team member/role responsible for running `gh secret set REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` before the PR is merged, reference the key-rotation cadence from the project's secret-management policy, and note that removing the workflow or migrating off bot-signing triggers secret deletion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit precondition or work item verifying that an identity ticket with `email: joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` already exists in the tickets store (or create it as part of this plan), so the git-email resolution path in `resolve_current_identity()` can succeed.
findings.finding: The plan asserts signing will happen automatically 'once identity + key are configured' and that the git-email flip 'maps to the Rebar Bot identity', but the code shows `resolve_current_identity()` requires either a use-pointer file OR an identity ticket in the store whose `email` field exactly matches `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`. The plan does not assert, verify, or provision that such an identity ticket already exists in the tickets store — an empirically checkable codebase precondition the plan leaves implicit.
findings.location: Plan body: 'so git-email resolution maps to the Rebar Bot identity'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a prerequisite step (or AC) confirming the 'Rebar Bot' identity ticket with email `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` exists in the tracker before signing can succeed, or specify that the plan includes creating it.
findings.finding: The 'Rebar Bot' identity ticket (with email `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`) must pre-exist in the tracker for `resolve_current_identity`'s email-match path to succeed and produce a signed event. The plan neither creates this ticket, verifies its existence, nor lists it as a prerequisite, yet AC2 asserts the canary-created event will be 'signed as Rebar Bot'.
findings.location: AC2: 'A canary-created bug/comment/transition is signed as Rebar Bot (verifiable via `verify-identity`)'; Plan body: 'so it will SIGN AUTOMATICALLY through `_seam.append_event` once identity + key are configured'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly whether the 'Rebar Bot' identity ticket (with email `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`) already exists in the store, or add a plan step to create/confirm it — without a resolvable identity, `resolve_current_identity()` returns None, `author_id` is absent from the event envelope, and `_seam.append_event` silently skips signing regardless of the injected key.
findings.finding: The signing mechanism in _seam.append_event fires only when author_id resolves — i.e., resolve_current_identity() returns non-None, which requires either an explicit identity-use pointer OR a matching identity ticket with email `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` in the store. The plan never specifies that this identity ticket exists or must be created first, so if it is absent, signing silently produces unsigned events and AC2 ('signed as Rebar Bot, verifiable via verify-identity') cannot be satisfied.
findings.location: Plan body: 'it will SIGN AUTOMATICALLY through _seam.append_event once identity + key are configured'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a plan step: 'Confirm a `rebar identity` ticket with email `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` exists in the store (or create one via `rebar identity create --name "Rebar Bot" --email joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com --self`). This is required for `resolve_current_identity()` to return the bot identity and populate `author_id` on the event envelope, which is the gating condition for signing in `_seam.append_event`.' Alternatively, add an explicit `rebar identity use <bot-ticket-id>` step in the workflow to use pointer-based resolution instead of email-match.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` holds an OpenSSH SSH private key (for `identity.signing_key` / in-toto authorship) or a UUID/HMAC key (for `REBAR_SIGNING_KEY`), and ensure the plan also covers registering the bot's corresponding public key in the identity keyring so `verify-identity` reports `verified` rather than `foreign_key`.
findings.finding: The plan conflates two separate signing subsystems: `REBAR_SIGNING_KEY` (env var feeding `signing.signing_key()` for HMAC manifest attestations, accepts raw UUID bytes) and `identity.signing_key` (config file-path to an OpenSSH SSH private key feeding `sign_event_authorship` for in-toto authorship). The plan says 'inject signing key to a job-local file; set identity.signing_key to it' — but does not specify what FORMAT the secret `REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` holds. If it is a UUID/HMAC key, passing it to `sign_event_authorship(key_path=...)` will fail at the OpenSSH layer; if it is an SSH private key, the plan must also address public-key registration in the bot identity's keyring or `verify-identity` will never return `verified`.
findings.location: Plan body: 'Inject secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY to a job-local file; set identity.signing_key to it.' / AC2: 'verifiable via verify-identity'
findings.suggested_fix: If using in-toto authorship signing: generate an SSH keypair for the bot, store the private key as `REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY`, and add a one-time step (outside the canary workflow, at identity setup time) to register the public key in the Rebar Bot identity's keyring. In the workflow, write the secret to a 0600 tmpfile and set `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY=<path>` (the documented env override for `identity.signing_key`) rather than modifying `rebar.toml` at runtime.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the prerequisite: a Rebar Bot identity ticket with email `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` must exist in the store (or be provisioned as part of this plan) for the git-email-match path in `resolve_current_identity()` to succeed and trigger signing.
findings.finding: The plan asserts signing fires 'AUTOMATICALLY through `_seam.append_event` once identity + key are configured', but the seam's decision rule requires `resolve_current_identity()` to return non-None (i.e., a matching identity ticket must exist in the store with the bot's email), which is a prerequisite the plan never specifies or names. The protocol for identity resolution — 'git email match only succeeds if a store-resident identity ticket carries the exact email `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`' — is referenced but not co-located with the plan's description of the signing trigger.
findings.location: Plan body — 'it will SIGN AUTOMATICALLY through `_seam.append_event` once identity + key are configured'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a work item: 'Confirm (or create) a Rebar Bot identity ticket carrying email `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` in the tracker store; document its ticket ID.' Reference it in AC2 as a precondition for the signed-as-bot verification.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
